title: Reading and Use of English - Part 7
description: You are going to read an article about four people who talk about doing home renovation or improvement projects. For questions 43–52, choose from the people (A–D). The people may be chosen more than once.

content.title: Making It Home: Four People on DIY and Home Improvement

texts.A:
### Nick, 47, Plumber
[43]I've been doing DIY for as long as I can remember – my father was a builder and he handed tools to me almost as soon as I could hold them.[/43] Most of the projects I take on at home are things I could technically pay someone else to do, but I get genuine satisfaction from having done them myself. [47]Last year I completely refitted my kitchen single-handed over three weekends, and walking into that room still gives me a sense of accomplishment that no professional job could replicate.[/47] The only projects I now approach with caution are electrical ones; I understand where my skills end and where someone else's should begin. [51]I've also started teaching my teenage daughter basic plumbing and carpentry, because I want her to feel capable in her own home.[/51]
texts.B:
### Julia, 38, Architect
Designing buildings professionally has given me very precise opinions about space and proportion, which makes home improvement both easier and more difficult. [44]I find it hard to start a project without detailed plans, and I've been known to produce full architectural drawings for what should have been a weekend decorating job.[/44] [48]The biggest project I undertook was converting our attic into a home office, which I designed myself but handed over to contractors for the build, as I knew my strengths lay in planning rather than physical construction.[/48] [52]What surprised me most was how many small decisions arise during a building project that no drawing can anticipate – improvisation is unavoidable even with the best preparation.[/52]
texts.C:
### Steve, 29, Delivery Driver
[45]I bought a rundown flat two years ago because it was all I could afford, and I've spent nearly every weekend since then trying to make it liveable.[/45] I had never picked up a paintbrush before in my life. I watched hundreds of online tutorials and made an enormous number of mistakes before anything started to look right. [49]The money I've saved by doing the work myself rather than hiring tradespeople has been substantial – easily over ten thousand pounds so far.[/49] I've also discovered that I genuinely enjoy it, which came as a complete surprise. I'm considering retraining as a carpenter because the work gives me a satisfaction that driving never has.
texts.D:
### Karen, 55, Retired Nurse
[46]When my children left home, the house suddenly felt too big and too empty, and I channelled that discomfort into a complete redecorating project.[/46] I stripped wallpaper I had lived with for twenty years, repainted every room, and replaced flooring in three of them over a single summer. [50]The physical effort involved was far more demanding than I had expected, and I ended up with aches I hadn't had since my busiest years as a nurse.[/50] But there was something cathartic about dismantling the old and building something new, particularly at a time of significant change in my family life. The house now feels genuinely mine rather than something that just accumulated around me.

questions.question: was introduced to practical skills at a very young age by a family member?
questions.explanation: Nick's father was a builder who handed tools to him almost as soon as he could hold them.

questions.question: finds it difficult to begin a home project without extensive planning?
questions.explanation: Julia finds it hard to start without detailed plans and has produced full architectural drawings for what should have been a simple weekend job.

questions.question: took on home improvement out of financial necessity rather than choice?
questions.explanation: Steve bought a rundown flat because it was all he could afford and has spent every weekend since making it liveable.

questions.question: began a significant home project in response to a change in their personal circumstances?
questions.explanation: Karen channelled the discomfort of her children leaving home into a complete redecorating project.

questions.question: describes the lasting satisfaction of completing a large-scale project entirely by themselves?
questions.explanation: Nick refitted his kitchen single-handed over three weekends, and walking into that room still gives him a sense of accomplishment.

questions.question: decided to delegate the physical work of a project while retaining control of the design?
questions.explanation: Julia designed the attic conversion herself but handed the build over to contractors, recognising her strengths lay in planning rather than construction.

questions.question: highlights a significant financial advantage gained from doing the work themselves?
questions.explanation: Steve estimates he has saved over ten thousand pounds by doing the work himself rather than hiring tradespeople.

questions.question: mentions being surprised by the physical demands of a home improvement project?
questions.explanation: Karen says the physical effort was far more demanding than expected, leaving her with aches she hadn't had since her busiest nursing years.

questions.question: mentions passing on practical skills to a younger member of their family?
questions.explanation: Nick has started teaching his teenage daughter basic plumbing and carpentry so she will feel capable in her own home.

questions.question: was surprised to discover that even careful preparation does not eliminate the need for flexibility?
questions.explanation: Julia was surprised by how many small decisions arise during a build that no drawing can anticipate, making improvisation unavoidable.
